name: Zahav
description: James Beard Award-winning Israeli restaurant from Michael Solomonov. The hummus alone is worth the trip — the whole lamb shoulder is legendary. Michelin Recommended.

name: Suraya
description: Lebanese restaurant, garden, and market in Fishtown. Beautiful outdoor garden, incredible mezze, and a weekend brunch that draws crowds. Michelin Recommended.

name: Vernick Food & Drink
description: Greg Vernick's polished-casual American spot in Rittenhouse. Known for the toast course and impeccable seasonal cooking. Michelin Recommended and 2026 James Beard semifinalist for Outstanding Restaurateur.

name: John's Roast Pork
description: No-frills stand slinging what many consider the best roast pork sandwich in Philly. Cash only, limited hours — get there early.

name: Laser Wolf
description: Rooftop Israeli grill from the Zahav team. All-you-can-eat salatim followed by skewers cooked over charcoal. The views don't hurt either. Michelin Recommended.

name: Bok Bar
description: Seasonal rooftop bar on top of the old Bok vocational school. Cheap drinks, incredible skyline views, and one of the best sunset spots in the city. Shares the 8th floor with Irwin's.

name: Hop Sing Laundromat
description: Philly's most exclusive speakeasy. No phones, no photos, strict dress code. World-class cocktails behind an unmarked door in Chinatown.

name: Dirty Franks
description: Legendary dive bar in Center City. Cheap PBR, a jukebox, and a crowd that ranges from artists to attorneys. A Philly institution since 1933.

name: Philadelphia Museum of Art
description: World-class art collection in one of the most iconic buildings in the city. Run the Rocky steps, then spend hours inside with everything from medieval armor to Duchamp.

name: The Barnes Foundation
description: One of the world's greatest collections of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist art. The gallery arrangement is as much art as the works themselves.

name: Wissahickon Valley Park
description: 1,800 acres of forested trails along the Wissahickon Creek. Feels like you left the city entirely. Forbidden Drive is the main artery — flat, scenic, perfect for a long walk.

name: Spruce Street Harbor Park
description: Seasonal waterfront park with hammocks, floating gardens, food vendors, and colorful lights. Perfect for a casual warm-weather hang on the Delaware.
address: 301 S Christopher Columbus Blvd, Philadelphia, PA 19106

name: Rittenhouse Square
description: The crown jewel of Philly parks. People-watch from a bench, grab coffee from a nearby café, or just sit in the grass on a nice day.

name: Reading Terminal Market
description: Historic indoor farmers market with 80+ vendors. DiNic's roast pork, Beiler's donuts, Bassett's ice cream — come hungry, leave happy.

name: Herman's Coffee
description: Cult-favorite coffee roaster, specialty market, and Friday/Saturday cocktail bar in a converted auto repair shop. In-house roasted beans, espresso tonics, imported tinned fish, natural wine, and merch by local artists. Best of Philly winner — twice.

name: Laurel
description: Intimate BYOB tasting menu in East Passyunk from chef Nicholas Elmi. Eight courses of refined French-inspired cooking in a room that seats about 22. Michelin Recommended.

name: Tired Hands Brewing
description: Cult-favorite brewery known for hazy IPAs and fruited sours. The Fishtown Fermentaria has a great taproom vibe.

name: R&D Cocktail Bar
description: Inventive cocktail bar in Northern Liberties. Science-meets-mixology with a rotating menu of creative drinks and a cozy back patio.

name: Eastern State Penitentiary
description: Crumbling, atmospheric historic prison turned museum. Once held Al Capone. The audio tour is excellent — and it's genuinely eerie.

name: Schuylkill River Trail
description: Paved trail running along the Schuylkill River. Great for running, biking, or a sunset walk with skyline views. Connects to Fairmount Park.

name: Vedge
description: Upscale vegan restaurant in a gorgeous old brownstone. Even die-hard carnivores leave impressed. The rutabaga fondue is iconic. Michelin Recommended.

name: Pat's King of Steaks
description: The original Philly cheesesteak since 1930. Outdoor-only, cash-friendly, and open 24/7. Order "whiz wit" like a local.

name: Magic Gardens
description: Isaiah Zagar's immersive mosaic art environment spanning half a city block on South Street. A dazzling labyrinth of glass, tile, and found objects.

name: Monk's Cafe
description: Belgian beer bar with one of the deepest beer lists on the East Coast. Mussels and frites are the move. A Rittenhouse institution.

name: Longwood Gardens
description: Spectacular botanical gardens just outside the city in Kennett Square. Massive conservatory, seasonal displays, fountain shows, and acres of manicured grounds.

name: The Bourse Food Hall
description: Beautifully renovated food hall in Old City with a curated mix of local vendors. Great lunch spot when exploring the historic district.
address: 111 S Independence Mall E, Philadelphia, PA 19106

name: The Mütter Museum
description: Fascinating (and sometimes unsettling) museum of medical oddities. Skulls, specimens, and the famous soap lady. Not for the squeamish.

name: Friday Saturday Sunday
description: Rittenhouse cocktail bar and restaurant with a Michelin star. Creative cocktails downstairs, a refined multicourse menu upstairs from chef Chad Williams. One of Philly's first three Michelin-starred restaurants.

name: Bartram's Garden
description: America's oldest living botanical garden on the banks of the Schuylkill. Free to visit, with community events, kayaking, and beautiful river views.

name: a]kitchen + bar
description: Trendy spot in the AKA Rittenhouse hotel. Great for a date — excellent cocktails, seasonal plates, and a sophisticated-but-relaxed vibe.

name: Sonny's Cocktail Joint
description: Come-as-you-are cocktail bar on South Street with a killer backyard, pool table, and draft cocktails including sub-zero freezer mixes. Bar pies, smash burgers, and the legendary chicken-fried shrimp. Best of Philly winner for Best Comeback — survived a fire and a flood and keeps coming back stronger.

name: Grace & Proper
description: Vintage corner EuroBar in the Italian Market from the Happy Monday Hospitality crew. Portuguese-inspired wine bar with bifana sandwiches, mortadella boards, bacalao, and towering piles of meat and truffle chips. Walk-ins only, no reservations — like a European corner bar should be.

name: Kalaya
description: Chef Nok Suntaranon's fiery southern Thai restaurant — Esquire's Best New Restaurant in America, James Beard Best Chef: Mid-Atlantic winner, and Michelin Recommended. The shaw muang dumplings are iconic. No pad thai on the menu, and you won't miss it.

name: Her Place Supper Club
description: Amanda Shulman's Michelin-starred supper club — half dinner party, half restaurant. The multicourse set menu changes every two weeks and everyone is served at the same time. Reservations drop on the third Sunday of each month and go fast.

name: Provenance
description: Chef Nicholas Bazik's Michelin-starred tasting menu — one of Philly's first three restaurants to earn the honor. Reservations open the first of each month and the waitlist runs about 50 deep. Precision cooking with high-wire ambition in a small, focused room.

name: Middle Child
description: The sandwich shop that became a Philly institution. Inspired by old-school delis, Jewish diners, and Italian hoagie shops. House-corned-beef sandwiches, blueberry-jam turkey subs, and vegan hoagies that actually slap. Two locations — the original in Wash West and the all-day Clubhouse in Fishtown.

name: Middle Child Clubhouse
description: Middle Child's all-day big sibling in Fishtown. Breakfast, lunch, and a full dinner program with a Noma-alum running the night kitchen. The brick chicken is a must. Full bar with great cocktails.

name: Irwin's
description: Modern Sicilian restaurant on the 8th floor of the Bok Building, named one of America's best new restaurants by both Esquire and Bon Appétit. Chef Michael Vincent Ferreri's handmade pasta, seasonal seafood, and natural wine list are stellar. The rooftop terrace has skyline views. Book Salvatore's Counter for a 10-course chef's experience at just 4 seats.

name: Forsythia
description: Chef Christopher Kearse's Old City French bistro — vibrant, creative, and Michelin Recommended. Tempura frog legs with saffron aioli caught the Michelin inspectors' attention. Seasonal ingredients meet uninhibited French technique.

name: Emilia
description: Greg Vernick and chef Meri Medoway's new Italian-inspired spot in Kensington. Handmade pastas like ragù bianco rigatoni, crispy veal, and an all-Italian wine and amari list. Casual neighborhood energy with serious cooking.

name: Dancerobot
description: Jesse Ito (of Royal Sushi fame, 8x James Beard nominee) and chef Justin Bacharach's high-energy Japanese restaurant. Katsu curry, playful cocktails, quality sake, and strong zero-proof options. The fun, loud alternative to an omakase.

name: Uchi
description: Tyson Cole's acclaimed Japanese restaurant chain lands in Philly. Non-traditional sushi and creative Japanese dishes in a cozy, intimate setting. Book via the restaurant's website directly.

name: The Bourse Food Hall
description: Beautifully renovated food hall in Old City with a curated mix of local vendors. Great lunch spot when exploring the historic district.
address: 111 S Independence Mall E, Philadelphia, PA 19106

name: Cry Baby Pasta
description: Colorful, rock-and-roll-themed Italian spot from the Bridget Foy's family and the ex-Koo Zee Doo kitchen team. Everything is made from scratch — the tortellini in pork-and-chicken brodo is crystalline perfection, and the rigatoni vodka sauce with chicken and pancetta ('chicken riggies') is a cult favorite. Nothing over $22, deep Italian wine list on tap, aperitivo hour 4–6pm at the bar and outdoor café.

name: Bridget Foy's
description: South Street corner institution since 1978 — family-owned, fire-tested, and still going strong. Comfort food classics, a solid burger, great happy hour, and one of the best outdoor patios on Headhouse Square. All-you-can-eat pasta Mondays for $30. The kind of neighborhood spot where regulars and tourists mix easily.

name: Ace Coffee Outpost
description: Part café, part vintage store, part event space on Fabric Row. Good coffee, raspberry sprinkle donuts, a projector playing skate videos, and regular pop-ups including wine nights from Mish Mish and block parties. Blends right in with the quirky shops on 4th Street.

name: Royal Sushi & Izakaya
description: Two restaurants in one — a walk-in izakaya up front with anime on the walls, $4 beers, and crispy karaage wings, plus a hidden 8-seat omakase counter in the back that's the hardest reservation in Philly. Chef Jesse Ito (8x James Beard nominee) runs the best sushi in the city. Michelin Bib Gourmand.

name: Ambra
description: One of the most special dining experiences in Philly — a 10-ish course Italian tasting menu at either a communal table or a 4-seat kitchen counter, attached to Southwark. Chef Chris D'Ambro cooks from childhood memories with truffle, caviar, and handmade pasta. $280–$300 per person, cash only. Reservations drop two months out on the 1st. Michelin Recommended.

name: Southwark
description: Handsome Queen Village corner tavern with one of the city's best cocktail programs — drinks named after movie lines — plus farm-to-table cooking, a killer double cheeseburger with local blue cheese, and a plant-filled back patio that's perfect for date night. Michelin Recommended. Same team as Ambra next door.

name: Tattooed Mom
description: South Street's most beloved dive bar and unofficial street art museum. Every surface is covered in 25 years of stickers, spray paint, and murals. Vegan-friendly bar food (Nashville hot 'chicken', vegan cheesesteaks), over-the-top cocktails with cotton candy and Pop Rocks, comedy shows, craft nights, and a bumper car in the entrance. Pure South Street chaos.

name: Famous 4th Street Deli
description: Old-school Jewish deli and Queen Village brunch institution. Enormous pastrami specials, challah french toast, matzah ball soup, and towering smoked fish platters. Gets loud and packed on weekends — worth the wait.

name: Farina Di Vita
description: Takeout-only sandwich shop making some of the best hoagies and chicken cutlet sandwiches on this side of Broad. The Papale Special (pepper turkey, Cooper Sharp, olive oil) is deceptively simple and perfect. Call ahead — they sell out early.

name: Royal Tavern
description: Queen Village neighborhood bar with surprisingly excellent late-night food that punches way above its weight class. The kind of place where you go for a beer and end up staying for the whole menu.

name: Queen & Rook Game Cafe
description: Board game bar on South Street with 2,000+ games, old-school arcade cabinets, and pinball machines. Nerdy cocktails from the 'Wizarding' menu, seitan wings, and nachos. Perfect for a group hang that doesn't revolve around just drinking.

name: Jim's South Street
description: Classic cheesesteak shop that draws a line around the block at any hour. Recently renovated and expanded after a fire. A South Street institution and legit contender for Philly's best steak.

name: Bloomsday
description: Great food, wine, and cocktails to be shared with friends… At Bloomsday, we believe in the simplicity of everyday pleasures. We are a female-led neighborhood restaurant, wine bar and bottle shop striving to make wine infinitely accessible. We don’t do fussy. And because people have always loved a good drink in the company of good food, we do that too. Stay a while or just stop in. Here on the cobblestone shambles of Headhouse Square in Philadelphia, Bloomsday is a lively meeting place for friends, neighbors, and those just passing through.

name: Sidecar Bar & Grille
description: A neighborhood gem with an impressive whiskey selection and solid pub grub that doesn't try too hard. The kind of place where you can settle in for the evening with a good bourbon and some wings without any pretense.

name: PHS Pop Up Garden at South Street
description: The Pennsylvania Horticultural Society transforms vacant lots and underutilized spaces into beautiful temporary gardens throughout the city. These colorful, community-focused spaces offer a peaceful escape and showcase how green infrastructure can revitalize neighborhoods.

name: Supérette
description: This Paris-meets-Passyunk wine bar is one of the most fun places to eat and drink in Philly right now. The zinc bar, natural light, and French market vibes make it feel like the perfect summer escape where everything should be paired with a glass of something orange and funky. It's the kind of place that makes you feel like you've come exactly where you were meant to be.

name: Le Caveau
description: This French natural wine bar in Bella Vista is a hidden gem with romantic, moody vibes and a 130-bottle wine list that's sustainably focused. The knowledgeable bartenders serve draft cocktails alongside French charcuterie and their famous French hot dog, all in a dimly lit space with exposed brick and tin ceilings. It's classy without being pretentious, making it perfect for an intimate night out.

name: Gilda Café & Market
description: This beloved Fishtown gem serves up some of the best breakfast sandwiches in the city alongside authentic Portuguese pastéis de nata that rival those found in Lisbon. The sunny European-countryside atmosphere and consistently exceptional food make it feel like catching up with friends every time you visit.

name: Bloomsday
description: This female-led neighborhood gem is everything you want in one spot - wine bar, restaurant, and bottle shop with a cozy, book-loving vibe. They make their own charcuterie, pickles, and even vermouths, plus the outdoor seating overlooking historic Headhouse Square is perfect for people watching. The staff actually knows their wine and the sound-baffles mean you can actually have a conversation - no fussy vibes here.

name: LMNO
description: This Stephen Starr concept is an absolute delight with some of the best vibes in Philly - it's a Baja-Mexican restaurant, art gallery, bookstore, and intimate music venue all under one roof. The millennial pink courtyard and gorgeously designed space make it both stylish and quirky-comfortable, plus their prickly pear margaritas are exceptional.

name: Three Graces Coffee Co.
description: This bright and cozy coffee shop in Queen Village is perfect for working or studying with its quiet atmosphere and excellent specialty drinks like their strawberry latte with real strawberry chunks. The space feels softer and more welcoming than typical industrial coffee shops, plus they're dog-friendly and provide homemade treats for your furry friends.

name: Middle Child
description: This everyday neighborhood joint serves up some seriously amazing sandwiches that even caught the New York Times' attention - their turkey sandwich with house-made blueberry jam on fresh ciabatta is apparently superb. Just be prepared for crowds and long waits, especially during weekend brunch, because this place has developed quite the local following for good reason.

name: Triangle Tavern
description: This South Philly institution since 1933 is the perfect blend of neighborhood tavern and Italian restaurant, serving everything from classic spaghetti and meatballs to innovative vegan versions. With a killer beer selection, spiked water ice, and that friendly local vibe, it's exactly what you want from an East Passyunk hangout.

name: O'Jung's Bar
description: This beloved neighborhood gastropub has been serving Pennsport for over 20 years with unbeatable weekly specials and some of the best wings in South Philly. It's definitely a local's bar with friendly bartenders, affordable drinks, and live weekend entertainment, though it can get pretty crowded during peak hours. Fair warning - it's cash and Venmo only, but there's an ATM on site.

name: Honeysuckle
description: This Michelin Guide-recommended fine dining spot is way more than just a restaurant - it's a cultural preservation space serving creative Caribbean and African-inspired dishes that tell stories through food. The multi-course tasting menu ($95) gets rave reviews for its beautiful presentation and thoughtful storytelling, though be prepared for a 3+ hour experience with some pacing issues.
